original_abstract: The preceding chapters have shown how, in a relatively short period of time, GCC governments were able to build a national system of public higher education alongside a variety of private institutions that have made higher education more accessible to those seeking it. This accessibility has been particularly beneficial to women who now can study at home compared to the past when few parents allowed their daughters to pursue higher education abroad. In addition, the children of expatriate workers, both males and females, who have grown up in the GCC no longer have to leave the region in order to receive a college degree.
humanized_abstract: After summarizing the earlier chapters, we move on to discuss how the GCC governments managed to establish a system for public higher education along with private sectors that increasingly provided more opportunities for citizens to access higher education. Increasing access to higher education has had positive effects not only on citizens but also on women, children of the expatriates working in the region and local children born to expatriate parents. As a result, women’s parents are now content with women studying at home. Children of expatriates who also grew up in the GCC countries can also graduate from universities in these countries.

As a result, women’s parents are now content with women studying at home. Children of expatriates who also grew up in the GCC countries can also graduate from universities in these countries.
